text: The surgeon uses robotic technology to close the abdominal defect, incorporating part of the hernia sac to eliminate the space between the skin and abdominal muscle. Strong, heavy sutures are used to close the defect in two layers, with reduced intra-abdominal pressure to minimize tension on the wound.
The surgeon places a mesh over the abdominal defect to reinforce the closure, addressing the significant tension due to the patient's obesity.

The surgeon inserts a two-layered mesh into the abdomen. The rough layer is positioned to attach to the abdominal wall, while the smoother hydrogel layer, which is absorbable, faces the intra-abdominal structures.

The surgeon uses special suturing techniques to attach the mesh securely to the abdominal wall, ensuring no intra-abdominal structures can become trapped between the mesh and the abdominal wall. Over two weeks, the abdominal wall incorporates the mesh into the musculature, forming a sheet over the mesh.

The surgeon carefully sutures the mesh to the abdominal wall, ensuring the rough side faces the wall and not the intra-abdominal structures. A ring in the middle of the mesh is visible, which will be removed later. The surgeon uses precise suturing techniques to avoid wrinkling the mesh while ensuring it is securely attached without excessive tension.
The surgeon removes the ring from the mesh through the port, showing it completely separated from the abdominal wall.

The surgeon places additional sutures in the middle of the mesh, securing it to the abdominal wall. The mesh is visibly attached without tension or wrinkles, ensuring it is completely secured to the abdominal wall muscle.
